question:
Let $S$ be the smallest set of positive integers such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)]
$2$ is in $S$,
\item[(b)]
$n$ is in $S$ whenever $n^2$ is in $S$, and
\item[(c)]
$(n+5)^2$ is in $S$ whenever $n$ is in $S$.
\end{enumerate}
Which positive integers are not in $S$?

(The set $S$ is ``smallest'' in the sense that $S$ is contained in any other such set.)

descriptive_long.question:
Let $minimalset$ be the smallest set of positive integers such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)]
$2$ is in $minimalset$,
\item[(b)]
$variableinteger$ is in $minimalset$ whenever $variableinteger^2$ is in $minimalset$, and
\item[(c)]
$(variableinteger+5)^2$ is in $minimalset$ whenever $variableinteger$ is in $minimalset$.
\end{enumerate}
Which positive integers are not in $minimalset$?

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $sunflower$ be the smallest set of positive integers such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)]
$2$ is in $sunflower$,
\item[(b)]
$passenger$ is in $sunflower$ whenever $passenger^2$ is in $sunflower$, and
\item[(c)]
$(passenger+5)^2$ is in $sunflower$ whenever $passenger$ is in $sunflower$.
\end{enumerate}
Which positive integers are not in $sunflower$?

(The set $sunflower$ is ``smallest'' in the sense that $sunflower$ is contained in any other such set.)

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $largestset$ be the smallest set of positive integers such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)]
$2$ is in $largestset$,
\item[(b)]
$nonnumber$ is in $largestset$ whenever $nonnumber^2$ is in $largestset$, and
\item[(c)]
$(nonnumber+5)^2$ is in $largestset$ whenever $nonnumber$ is in $largestset$.
\end{enumerate}
Which positive integers are not in $largestset$?

garbled_string.question:
Let $vmdlqsrp$ be the smallest set of positive integers such that
\begin{enumerate}
\item[(a)]
$2$ is in $vmdlqsrp$,
\item[(b)]
$qzxwvtnp$ is in $vmdlqsrp$ whenever $qzxwvtnp^2$ is in $vmdlqsrp$, and
\item[(c)]
$(qzxwvtnp+5)^2$ is in $vmdlqsrp$ whenever $qzxwvtnp$ is in $vmdlqsrp$.
\end{enumerate}
Which positive integers are not in $vmdlqsrp$?

(The set $vmdlqsrp$ is ``smallest'' in the sense that $vmdlqsrp$ is contained in any other such set.)

kernel_variant.solution:
1.  Parity.
The only element placed in $S$ at the start is $3$, which is odd.  
Rules (b) and (c) both preserve parity:  
- If $n$ is even (resp.	hinspace odd) then $n^{2}$ is even (resp.	hinspace odd);  
- If $n$ is even (resp.	hinspace odd) then $n+4$ is even (resp.	hinspace odd), so $(n+4)^{2}$ has the same parity.  
Consequently every number ever produced from $3$ is odd.  Hence

          every even positive integer is **not** in $S$.

2.  A useful closure property.
Combining (c) and (b) gives
        $n\in S\;\Longrightarrow\;(n+4)^{2}\in S\;\Longrightarrow\;n+4\in S.$   (\star )
Thus $S$ is closed under repeatedly adding $4$.
In particular $3\in S$ produces
        $3,7,11,15,19,\dots$;
so every integer that is $\equiv 3\pmod 4$ lies in $S$.

3.  Getting a first square that is $1\pmod 4$.
Applying (c) to $3$ gives $(3+4)^{2}=7^{2}=49\in S$.  By (\star ),
        $49,53,57,61,\dots$ are also in $S$.
Among these is $81=49+8\cdot4$, whence $81\in S$.

4.  A smaller element enters.
Because $81=9^{2}$ and $81\in S$, rule (b) forces
        $9\in S$.

5.  From $9$ we descend to $5$.
Applying (\star ) to $9$ four times yields
        $9\rightarrow 13\rightarrow 17\rightarrow 21\rightarrow 25$.
Now $25=5^{2}\in S$, so rule (b) gives
        $5\in S$.

6.  All odd integers $\ge 3$ are in $S$.
Starting with $5\in S$, the closure (\star ) supplies every integer
        $5,9,13,17,\dots$, i.e. all odd integers that are $\equiv 1\pmod 4$ and at least $5$.
Together with the $\equiv 3\pmod 4$ integers produced in Step2 we obtain **every odd integer $\ge 3$.**

7.  Why $1$ never appears.
The only rule that can produce a *smaller* number is (b).  It converts
$n^{2}$ to $n$.  For (b) to create $1$ we would need $1^{2}=1$ already in
$S$, but $1$ was not given initially and cannot arise from (c) or (\star )
(these always create numbers $\ge 49$ and numbers larger than the start
respectively).  Hence $1\notin S$.

8.  Description of $S$ and of its complement.
From the preceding steps
        $S=\{\text{all odd positive integers}\}\setminus\{1\}.$
Therefore the positive integers **not** in $S$ are
        $1$ and every even positive integer.

9.  Minimality check.
Let $T$ be any set satisfying (a)(c).  Step1 shows that $T$ contains only odd integers, so all even numbers are excluded from every such set.  The argument of Steps26 is constructive and works inside any set satisfying (a)(c); hence every such set must contain all odd numbers $\ge 3$.  As argued in Step7 no such set can contain $1$.  Thus the set we have identified is contained in every other admissible set and is therefore the required smallest set.  \(\Box\)

core_steps: From (b)+(c) derive closure:  n∈S ⇒ n+5∈S (call this property (d)).
Propose candidate set S* = ℕ \ {1 and all multiples of 5}; verify (a),(b),(c) using mod-5 check.
Use the seed 2 together with (b),(c),(d) to build at least one number in every allowed residue class mod 5 (e.g. get 3,4,6).
Property (d) then fills out all numbers ≡2,3,4 (mod 5); hence any set satisfying (a)–(c) contains S*.
Therefore the integers not in S are exactly 1 and the multiples of 5.

slot1.description: Translation constant in rule (c), i.e. the '+5' that defines the relevant modulus.

slot2.description: Designated starting element supplied by condition (a).  It only needs to avoid the excluded residue classes.

slot3.description: Isolated excluded integer that is not a multiple of the translation constant.

slot4.description: Entire residue class excluded from S (here, the multiples of the translation constant).

slot5.description: Specific numerical chain chosen to exhibit representatives of the allowed residue classes (e.g. 49, 54², 56², 56, 121, 11, 16, 4, 9, 3, 36, 6). Any alternative chain achieving the same representatives would work equally well.
